parts.content: Distinguish between the perspectives of a **Cornucopian** and a **Deep Ecologist** regarding the management of global freshwater scarcity.
parts.markscheme:
Distinguish between the perspectives of a **Cornucopian** and a **Deep Ecologist** regarding the management of global freshwater scarcity.

- **Cornucopian perspective:**
  - Believes that technological innovation will always provide solutions to resource depletion (e.g., large-scale desalination/wastewater recycling)<marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Views the environment as a resource to be exploited for economic growth/human benefit  <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Deep Ecologist perspective:**
  - Argues that nature/water has intrinsic value regardless of its utility to humans<marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Advocates for a radical reduction in human consumption/population to live within the natural limits of the ecosystem  <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Do not accept general definitions of EVSs that are not applied to the context of water scarcity. Comparison must be explicit or clearly implied to gain full marks.</note>

<marks>4 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Significant historical events have played a crucial role in shaping the modern environmental movement. Outline how **three** named historical influences have shifted global environmental value systems (EVSs).
parts.markscheme:
Significant historical events have played a crucial role in shaping the modern environmental movement. Outline how **three** named historical influences have shifted global environmental value systems (EVSs).

- **Silent Spring by Rachel Carson (1962):** Raised public awareness about the dangers of chemical pesticides (DDT) / highlighted the concept of bioaccumulation / catalyzed the ban of certain toxins <marks>2 marks</marks>
- **Bhopal Disaster (1984):** A gas leak in India that caused thousands of deaths / raised international concern regarding industrial safety, corporate responsibility, and the vulnerability of developing nations <marks>2 marks</marks>
- **Chernobyl Nuclear Disaster (1986):** A reactor meltdown in the USSR that released radioactive material across Europe / sparked a global debate on the safety of nuclear energy and the transboundary nature of pollution <marks>2 marks</marks>
- **Minamata Disaster (1950s):** Mercury poisoning in Japan caused by industrial waste / demonstrated the link between industrial pollution and human health/neurological damage  <marks>2 marks</marks>
- **Gaia Hypothesis (1970s):** James Lovelock’s idea that Earth is a self-regulating organism / encouraged a more holistic/ecocentric view of the planet <marks>2 marks</marks>

<note>Award 1 mark for the name of the event and 1 mark for its specific impact on the environmental movement/EVS. Max 7 marks including 1 mark for clearly articulating the general shift from apathy to concern.</note>

parts.content: Outline **two** ways in which an individual’s environmental value system (EVS) may be influenced by their religious or spiritual beliefs.
parts.markscheme:
- **Concept of Stewardship**: Many religions (e.g., Christianity, Islam) teach that humans are caretakers of the Earth; this leads to an anthropocentric or ecocentric view where humans have a moral responsibility to protect nature for a higher power.
- **Dominion/Mastery**: Some interpretations of religious texts suggest humans have authority over nature; this may foster a more technocentric or strongly anthropocentric view that resources are provided solely for human use and exploitation.
- **Intrinsic value in Animism/Buddhism**: Beliefs that all living things have a soul or possess inherent spirit; this often results in a deeply ecocentric EVS where the rights of nature are equal to or greater than human needs.
- **Principles of Asceticism/Simple Living**: Many spiritual traditions emphasize non-materialism; this influences an EVS to prioritize reduced consumption and living within the limits of the environment (ecocentrism).
- **Sacred landscapes**: Specific geographical features (mountains, rivers) may be considered holy; this leads to localized environmental protection and a value system that prioritizes conservation over industrial development.

<Note> Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying a specific religious/spiritual concept and <marks>1 mark</marks> for outlining how it influences the environmental perspective/value system. <marks>4 max</marks>

parts.content: Explain how the 1950s Minamata disaster in Japan contributed to the development of the modern environmental movement and shifted societal perspectives on industrial progress.
parts.markscheme:
- **Health-Environment Link**: It clearly demonstrated the direct link between industrial waste (mercury) and severe human health consequences (neurological damage/death), moving the environmental debate from aesthetics to survival.
- **Bioaccumulation/Biomagnification**: It provided a real-world case study of how toxins concentrate as they move up the food chain, highlighting the dangers of persistent pollutants.
- **Corporate Accountability**: The long struggle for compensation from the Chisso Corporation highlighted the need for legal frameworks to hold industries responsible for environmental damage.
- **Grassroots Activism**: The movement by victims and their families showed the power of local communities to challenge powerful industrial interests, a hallmark of the modern movement.
- **Skepticism of Technocentrism**: It caused a shift in the public EVS, creating doubt that industrial technology and 'progress' were inherently beneficial or safe without regulation.
- **Global Policy Impact**: The tragedy eventually led to international cooperation, culminating in the Minamata Convention on Mercury, which regulates mercury use globally.
- **Media Influence**: Photographic evidence (e.g., by W. Eugene Smith) brought the suffering to a global audience, demonstrating the role of media in shaping environmental values.
- **Interconnectedness**: It illustrated that local pollution in a marine ecosystem (Minamata Bay) has far-reaching consequences for the entire social and biological community.
- **Regulatory Shift**: Encouraged governments to move away from laissez-faire industrial policy toward precautionary principles and stricter effluent monitoring.

<Note> Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each valid explanatory point, up to a maximum of <marks>7 marks</marks>.

parts.content: To what extent are technocentric and ecocentric approaches different in their management of global biodiversity loss?
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:
- **Understanding of EVS concepts**: 
  - Technocentrism (focus on technology, management, and the idea that human ingenuity can solve any problem).
  - Ecocentrism (focus on the intrinsic value of nature, the need for humans to live within limits, and habitat preservation).
- **Comparison of strategies**:
  - **Technocentric**: Genetic engineering (CRISPR), de-extinction projects, seed banks (Svalbard), satellite monitoring of poaching, high-tech intensive agriculture to reduce land use (vertical farming).
  - **Ecocentric**: Creation of large-scale wilderness preserves, restoration ecology, community-led conservation, education focusing on the intrinsic right of species to exist, promotion of veganism/reduced consumption to lower land demand.
- **Balanced analysis**:
  - Technocentrism provides immediate, scalable solutions to save specific species but may ignore the underlying causes of habitat destruction.
  - Ecocentrism addresses the root cause (human consumption/growth) but is often slow to implement and difficult to achieve in a globalized capitalist economy.
  - Both may agree on the need for 'protected areas,' but for different reasons (resource management vs. intrinsic rights).

| Marks | Level descriptor |
|-------|-----------------|
| 0 | The response does not reach a standard described by the descriptors below and is not relevant to the question. |
| 1–3 | The response contains: • minimal evidence of knowledge and understanding of ESS issues or concepts • fragmented knowledge statements poorly linked to the context of the question • some appropriate use of ESS terminology • no examples where required, or examples with insufficient explanation/relevance • superficial analysis that amounts to no more than a list of facts/ideas • judgments/conclusions that are vague or not supported by evidence/argument. |
| 4–6 | The response contains: • some evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts • knowledge statements effectively linked to the context of the question • largely appropriate use of ESS terminology • some use of relevant examples where required, but with limited explanation • clear analysis that shows a degree of balance • some clear judgments/conclusions, supported by limited evidence/arguments. |
| 7–9 | The response contains: • substantial evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts • a wide breadth of knowledge statements effectively linked with each other, and to the context of the question • consistently appropriate and precise use of ESS terminology • effective use of pertinent, well-explained examples, where required, showing some originality • thorough, well-balanced, insightful analysis • explicit judgments/conclusions that are well-supported by evidence/arguments and that include some critical reflection. |

questions.specification: Population ecologists use the concept of carrying capacity to describe limits to population growth in an environment.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.1 Individuals, populations, communities and ecosystems > 2.1D Carrying capacity
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.1D Carrying capacity
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question directly asks for the definition of 'carrying capacity' and the challenges in estimating it, which is the specific subject of leaf topic 2.1D.

parts.content: Outline the meaning of the term *carrying capacity* in ecology.
parts.markscheme:
- The maximum number of individuals of a species that an area/habitat/environment can sustainably support (over time), given available limiting factors/resources <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reference to long-term/sustainable support (not a temporary maximum) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reference to limiting factors/resources (e.g. food, space, water) determining the limit <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correctly links carrying capacity to population size/density for a given species in a given environment <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss why estimating carrying capacity for a population can be difficult.
parts.markscheme:
- Carrying capacity is not fixed; it varies over time as environmental conditions change (e.g. climate variability, disturbance, disease, introduced species) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Multiple limiting factors may operate simultaneously, and the limiting factor may change with conditions (Liebig’s law not always straightforward in natural systems) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Interactions between species (competition, predation, parasitism) can alter effective carrying capacity and are hard to quantify <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Resource availability may fluctuate seasonally/annually, making long-term averages uncertain <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Population needs/traits may change over time due to adaptation/evolution, altering resource demand or tolerance limits <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Estimating a precise relationship between population size and a given environmental factor often requires extensive/long-term data <marks>1 mark</marks>
- For humans: technology, trade and substitution of resources can rapidly change effective carrying capacity <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Conclusion: because carrying capacity depends on variable resources, interacting limiting factors and changing human/natural systems, any estimate is uncertain and context-dependent <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the usefulness of carrying capacity as a concept for managing human populations and natural ecosystems.
parts.markscheme:
0 marks: The response is not relevant to the question or does not reach a standard described by the descriptors below.

1–3 marks: States limited or vague points about carrying capacity; little or no evaluation; weak or no examples; limited use of ESS terminology; conclusions absent or unsupported.

4–6 marks: Explains some ways carrying capacity can inform management (e.g. sustainable yield, stocking rates, protected area planning) and some limitations (e.g. variability, uncertainty, human technology/trade); uses some appropriate terminology; includes at least one relevant example; offers a limited but supported judgement.

7–9 marks: Provides a balanced, well-developed evaluation of usefulness and limitations for both ecosystems and humans; integrates uncertainty, temporal variability, interacting limiting factors and socio-economic/technological change; uses precise ESS terminology and well-explained examples (e.g. fisheries collapse/maximum sustainable yield; grazing pressure and desertification; water-limited city/region and imports/technology); reaches a clear, well-supported overall judgement on when carrying capacity is most/least useful for management.

parts.content: Evaluate the role of a transition toward a circular economy in maintaining the integrity of natural capital while meeting the demands of a growing human population.
parts.markscheme:
- <marks>1 mark</marks> for clear definitions and use of terminology: natural capital (the stock of resources), natural income (the yield/flow from that stock), linear economy (take-make-dispose), and circular economy (restorative and regenerative by design).

- <marks>1-2 marks</marks> for breadth in addressing the mechanisms of a circular economy:
  - Decoupling economic growth from resource consumption.
  - Designing out waste and pollution (e.g., closed-loop systems).
  - Keeping products and materials in use (reuse, remanufacture, recycling).
  - Regenerating natural systems instead of just extracting from them.

- <marks>1-2 marks</marks> for addressing the preservation of natural capital:
  - Reducing the rate of depletion of non-renewable stocks.
  - Ensuring harvesting rates of renewable resources do not exceed natural income/sustainable yield.
  - Reducing the 'waste' component of the ecological footprint, preventing ecosystem degradation.

- <marks>1 mark</marks> for specific examples/strategies:
  - Product-as-a-service models (e.g., leasing rather than owning appliances).
  - Biological cycles (composting/anaerobic digestion returning nutrients to soil).
  - Technical cycles (upcycling rare earth metals from electronics).

- <marks>1-2 marks</marks> for balanced analysis of challenges and limitations:
  - The Second Law of Thermodynamics (energy is always lost as heat, and materials degrade over time/recycling cycles).
  - High initial costs and the need for global systemic change in infrastructure.
  - Jevons Paradox (increased efficiency may lead to increased total consumption).
  - Difficulty in tracking international supply chains.

- <marks>1 mark</marks> for a conclusion supported by the analysis, e.g., "While a circular economy offers a significant pathway to sustainability by mimicking natural nutrient cycles, its success depends heavily on global cooperation and the ability to reduce absolute consumption levels, rather than just increasing efficiency."

parts.content: Outline **four** distinct categories of ecosystem services identified by the Millennium Ecosystem Assessment (MA) that provide natural income to humanity.
parts.markscheme:
*   **Provisioning services:** The products obtained from ecosystems, such as food, fresh water, timber, fiber, and genetic resources.
*   **Regulating services:** The benefits obtained from the regulation of ecosystem processes, such as climate regulation, flood control, water purification, and pollination.
*   **Cultural services:** Non-material benefits people obtain through spiritual enrichment, cognitive development, recreation, and aesthetic experiences.
*   **Supporting services:** Services necessary for the production of all other ecosystem services, such as soil formation, nutrient cycling, and primary production.

<Note> Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each of the following categories and a brief description; <marks>4 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Explain the process and limitations of using Environmental Impact Assessments (EIAs) as a tool for ensuring the sustainable management of a proposed large-scale development project.
parts.markscheme:
- <marks>1 mark</marks> for the initial stage: Establishing a baseline study to assess the existing environmental conditions (abiotic and biotic) before development begins.
- <marks>1 mark</marks> for predicting impacts: Identifying potential positive and negative effects on the environment, society, and economy.
- <marks>1 mark</marks> for mitigation: Proposing measures to avoid, reduce, or compensate for negative impacts (e.g., creating habitat corridors or using water filtration).
- <marks>1 mark</marks> for decision-making: Providing a formal document that allows authorities to decide if the project should proceed based on sustainability criteria.
- <marks>1 mark</marks> for a limitation regarding data/prediction: Complexities in ecosystems make it difficult to predict long-term or indirect effects (e.g., bioaccumulation or trophic cascades).
- <marks>1 mark</marks> for a limitation regarding implementation: Lack of standardization across borders or inadequate post-development monitoring to ensure mitigation steps are followed.
- <marks>1 mark</marks> for a limitation regarding bias/influence: Developers may exert political or economic pressure on the assessment process, or the public's voice may be ignored in favor of economic gain.

<note>Award [4 max] if only the process is described or only limitations are described. A full 7 marks requires a balance of how the tool works and why it might fail to ensure sustainability.</note>

parts.content: To what extent are modern agricultural practices more effective than traditional practices in meeting the demands of the growing human population?
parts.markscheme:
- <marks>1 mark</marks> for a general statement about the need to increase food production to meet the demands of a growing population
  - e.g. "With the world's population increasing, there is a need to produce more food"

- <marks>2 marks</marks> for describing how modern agricultural practices can increase food production
  - e.g. "Modern practices such as the use of fertilizers, pesticides, and high-yield crop varieties can increase crop yields"
  - "Mechanization and large-scale farming allow for more efficient cultivation of larger areas"

- <marks>2 marks</marks> for describing how traditional agricultural practices may be less effective in meeting food demands
  - e.g. "Traditional practices like crop rotation and organic farming may have lower yields"
  - "Smaller-scale traditional farming is less efficient and cannot meet the demands of a large population"

- <marks>2 marks</marks> for evaluating the sustainability and environmental impacts of modern and traditional practices
  - e.g. "However, modern practices can have negative environmental impacts like soil degradation and pollution"
  - "Traditional practices are often more sustainable and environmentally friendly"

- <marks>2 marks</marks> for a well-reasoned and balanced conclusion
  - e.g. "While modern practices can increase food production, a combination of modern and traditional techniques may be necessary to meet demands sustainably"

<marks>9 marks in total</marks>

questions.specification:
**Table 1:** Estimated Annual Global Carbon Budget (approximate annual averages).

| Component | Flow Type | Quantity ($GtC \text{ yr}^{-1}$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Fossil Fuel Combustion | Input to Atmosphere | 9.4 |
| Land-Use Change (e.g., Deforestation) | Input to Atmosphere | 1.5 |
| Terrestrial Vegetation Uptake | Output from Atmosphere | 3.2 |
| Ocean Absorption | Output from Atmosphere | 2.6 |

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.3B Carbon cycle
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question uses a global carbon budget table (inputs/outputs to the atmosphere) and asks for net atmospheric accumulation and implications of changing carbon sinks/sources—core carbon cycle content.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2D Models

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 6.2C Climate models (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The limitation/prediction aspect also overlaps with climate modelling concepts (scale, uncertainty), though the prompt is framed as a carbon budget model rather than full climate models.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.1C Oceans as a carbon sink (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) concerns increased ocean absorption of carbon and its environmental implications, aligning with oceans as a carbon sink.

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) asks for an economic consequence of continued atmospheric carbon accumulation, linking climate change to economic impacts within environmental economics concepts.

parts.content: Calculate the net annual accumulation of carbon in the atmosphere based on the data in Table 1.
parts.markscheme:
$$(9.4 + 1.5) - (3.2 + 2.6) = 5.1 \text{ GtC yr}^{-1}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept $5.1$. Units are not required but must be correct if used.

parts.content: Identify two processes from the table that involve a transformation of matter or energy.
parts.markscheme:
- Photosynthesis / Terrestrial Vegetation Uptake (transforms light energy to chemical energy / inorganic $$CO_2$$ to organic biomass)
- Combustion of Fossil Fuels (transforms chemical energy to heat/light / organic carbon to inorganic $$CO_2$$)
- Respiration (if linked to land-use change/decomposition)
- Calcification (if linked to ocean processes)

<Note> Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each of the following, <marks>2 marks max</marks>.

parts.content: Outline one economic consequence of the continued accumulation of atmospheric carbon.
parts.markscheme:
- Increased costs for disaster relief/infrastructure repair due to extreme weather events
- Financial losses in the agricultural sector due to changing rainfall patterns/droughts
- Rising insurance premiums in high-risk coastal or fire-prone areas
- Costs associated with building coastal defenses/sea walls against rising sea levels
- Loss of revenue from the tourism industry (e.g., bleaching of coral reefs/loss of ski resorts)

<note>Accept any other reasonable economic response. Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for any one

parts.content: Outline one environmental implication of the "Ocean Absorption" flow increasing significantly.
parts.markscheme:
- Ocean acidification / decrease in pH of seawater
- Reduced availability of carbonate ions for calcifying organisms (e.g., corals, shellfish)
- Disruption of marine food webs due to loss of sensitive species
- Potential for the ocean to reach a tipping point where it becomes a source rather than a sink

<note>Any one. Do not accept just "global warming" or "sea level rise" as these are atmospheric/thermal effects, not direct consequences of the chemical absorption flow itself.

parts.content: Outline one limitation of using a global carbon model to predict specific local environmental changes.
parts.markscheme:
- Global models lack the resolution/detail to account for local microclimates or topography
- Data for certain regions (e.g., deep oceans or remote rainforests) may be incomplete or estimated
- High level of uncertainty in the magnitude of regional feedback mechanisms
- Human behavior and policy changes are unpredictable and can alter the system's trajectory

<Note> Any one

questions.specification:
**Urban Agriculture in London**
* 9% of London's area is farmland, mostly in the outer green belt
* New focus on vertical farms and rooftop gardens with beekeeping

**Vertical Farming Details:**
* Located underground and in former industrial sites
* Grows salad greens, lettuce, spinach and herbs
* Uses 70% less water and no pesticides versus traditional farms

**London's Bees:**
* 1000+ beekeepers operate in the city
* Higher bee density than rural areas
* City bees are healthier with more diverse food options
* North London features an 11 km wildflower "bee corridor"

**Figure 1: Vertical farm**
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/bf35f22a-adce-4787-8797-1aeb0abf4697)


**Figure 2: Rooftop beekeeping**

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/c59311ba-4f2d-401b-bdc6-3dc15af96f0f)

parts.content: State **one** benefit and **one** drawback of the ecological footprint as a model for measuring sustainability.
parts.markscheme:
### Benefit of the ecological footprint model <marks>1 max</marks>

- Good visualizing tool/easily understood by non-specialists
- Environmental indicator/hazard warning indicator
- Widespread use, so easy to compare with other areas
- Highlights problem areas/identifies necessary changes in lifestyle
- Iconic symbol for raising awareness
- Information can be used to inform policy by governments

### Drawback of the ecological footprint model <marks>1 max</marks>

- Calculations are complex/all models are simplifications
- Needs huge amounts of data/uses proxy data/estimates
- Land has more than one function
- Less reliable at local level/not everyone in the region has the same lifestyle
- Can be interpreted differently by cultures
- Does not show types of resources used
- Does not include all factors that contribute to sustainability

<note>Do not accept only 'it is inaccurate/does not use outliers'. Do not accept 'does not account for changes over time'.</note>

parts.content: State **one** factor that would allow a region’s ecological footprint to exceed its biocapacity.
parts.markscheme:
- Importing resources/food
- Exporting waste products

<marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Referencing both figures above, suggest how urban agriculture can increase London's carrying capacity
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each of the following; <marks>3 marks max</marks>

- Increases food resources, (which increases carrying capacity) <marks>1</marks>
- Decreases water use, (which increases carrying capacity) <marks>1</marks>
- No pesticides, so less water pollution/does not harm/pollute environment / no pesticides used so fewer inputs/resources needed, (which increases carrying capacity) <marks>1</marks>
- Vertical farming uses less land area, (so more land area is available for housing) <marks>1</marks>
- Farming on roofs and in abandoned buildings means more land area is available (for housing/population growth) <marks>1</marks>
- Bees result in more pollination, so more primary productivity, (increasing carbon sink) <marks>1</marks>

<note>Do not accept only 'bees increase resources available' without reference to increasing food.</note>

<note>Do not accept 'bees provide healthier food'.</note>

<note>Do not accept only 'no pesticides used / provides goods / produces agricultural items / pesticides cause eutrophication'.</note>
